We apply DGNA on NVIDIA's A100 and H100 GPUs, revealing NUMA node architecture, SM-NUMA relationships, and NUMA-aware memory allocation strategies used to maintain cache coherence. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first paper to detail the NUMA architecture within the GPU memory subsystem.

</details>


### [60] [Formal Foundations for Known Good Reliable Die Screening in Chiplet-Based AI Systems-on-Chip](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.20141)
*Prashanthi Metku,Chandra Gandu*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper introduces a transition from Known Good Die (KGD) to Known Good Reliable Die (KGRD) screening using a Bayesian probabilistic risk model and associated techniques. It addresses post-assembly reliability ensured via quantified failure probabilities and validated through Monte Carlo simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of existing Known Good Die (KGD) methodology which does not guarantee reliability or lifetime assurance post-assembly in chiplet-based AI systems-on-chip.

Method: The paper utilizes a Bayesian probabilistic risk model, safety-gated decision architecture, Bayes-optimal uncertainty-aware disposition boundaries, and a constrained closed-loop feedback mechanism to assess pre-assembly data for post-assembly reliability.

Result: The proposed framework provides post-assembly failure probability guarantees, maintains model improvement under reliability constraints, and performs consistently across tested gated thresholds according to Monte Carlo simulations.

Conclusion: The approach bridges the gap in semiconductor testing methodologies by formalizing risk and reliability assurance from die pre-assembly to post-assembly testing with validated theoretical properties.

Abstract: The rapid growth of chiplet-based artificial intelligence systems-on-chip (SoCs) has exposed a fundamental gap in semiconductor test methodology. Existing Known Good Die (KGD) screening guarantees pre-assembly functional correctness, yet it offers no probabilistic assurance of post-assembly reliability lifetime. To address this limitation, the present work formalizes the transition from KGD to Known Good Reliable Die (KGRD) screening as a constrained inference problem over incomplete pre-assembly observability. Building upon this formulation, four interlocking contributions are presented: (i) a Bayesian probabilistic risk model that maps pre-assembly telemetry to post-assembly failure likelihood with a quantified observability bias bound; (ii) a safety-gated decision architecture that provides a provable post-assembly failure probability guarantee; (iii) uncertainty-aware disposition boundaries derived from Bayes-optimal decision theory; and (iv) a constrained closed-loop feedback mechanism that delivers consistent model improvement without violating reliability constraints. A Monte Carlo simulation study on N = 4,000 synthetic dies verifies all four theoretical properties and confirms that the safety guarantee holds uniformly across the full range of tested gate threshold.

</details>


<div id='cs.CL'></div>

# cs.CL [[Back]](#toc)

### [61] [Stateful Guardrails for Multi-Turn LLM Systems: A Conversational Risk Accumulation Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19361)
*Sanjay Mishra,Divya Chukkapalli,Ganesh R. Naik*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper identifies a critical issue termed Conversational Risk Accumulation (CRA) in large language models (LLMs), where gradual risk arises over prolonged dialogue rather than isolated interactions. A session-layer framework and benchmarks are introduced to detect and evaluate this phenomenon.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the shortcomings of isolated prompt-response evaluation in LLMs, which fail to capture risks that emerge over the course of a dialogue, leading to the accumulation of harmful outcomes.

Method: The authors propose a session-layer CRA Framework that tracks semantic drift, information accumulation, and compliance gradient across a dialogue. They introduce CRA-Net DA, a model trained to handle trajectory scoring. Three benchmarks, CRA-Bench v0.1, v0.2, and an extended 5-family set, are developed alongside a trajectory-focused evaluation protocol.

Result: The proposed methods provide a structured approach to assess conversational risks using trajectory-native evaluation metrics and benchmarks, demonstrating capability in detecting within-distribution risks.

Conclusion: The paper underscores the importance of addressing dialogue-level risks in LLMs and provides tools, data, and protocols to benchmark and mitigate CRA effectively. This is a step toward safer conversational AI.

Abstract: Most safety guardrails for large language models (LLMs) evaluate each prompt-response pair in isolation, which misses failures that arise only over a dialogue as benign turns compose into harm. We term this Conversational Risk Accumulation (CRA): gradual intent drift, fragmented assembly of prohibited instructions, and sensitivity build-up from repeated disclosures. We propose a session-layer CRA Framework that tracks three trajectory signals: semantic drift from a session anchor, a sensitivity-weighted information accumulation graph over extracted entities, and a compliance-gradient signal capturing increasing willingness to comply. For scoring, we provide (i) an unsupervised convex fusion for attribution and ablations, and (ii) CRA-Net DA, a compact learned trajectory model trained with family-adversarial objectives to reduce length and topic-coverage confounds. To benchmark CRA, we release CRA-Bench v0.1 (1,200 eight-turn sessions across three threat families with topic-matched benign twins), CRA-Bench v0.2 (LLM-paraphrased variants to reduce template artifacts), and an extended 5-family set (2,000 sessions adding persona priming and context stuffing). We introduce a trajectory-native evaluation protocol with session-level splits, mixed-set threshold calibration, Trajectory AUROC, turns-to-detection, calibrated false-positive metrics, bootstrap confidence intervals, leave-one-family-out diagnostic stress tests, and synthetic-to-human transfer checks. Claims focus on within-distribution session scoring on CRA-Bench and human-transfer subsets.

</details>


### [62] [Learning the Arabic Dialect Continuum as a Continuous Space: A Regression Approach to Speaker Origin Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19751)
*Mohamed Aziz Khadraoui,Adel Ammar,Bilel Benjdira,Zahid Khan,Skander Turki,Wadii Boulila*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces a regression-based, continuous-space approach for geolocating Arabic dialects using advanced neural architectures, attaining a 481.2 km median error and supporting the Arabic dialect continuum hypothesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to refine Arabic dialect geolocation by modeling it on a continuous geographic scale instead of traditional discrete classifications, aiming for a more granular and accurate representation.

Method: The authors designed a hierarchical neural network combining XLS-R-300M and Whisper-large-v3 representations with phonotactic data, utilizing a Transformer encoder and attention pooling. A spherical geodesic loss was applied to optimize prediction in geographic terms.

Result: The model achieved a median localization error of 481.2 km and up to 64.5% accuracy for country prediction, validating the continuous modeling approach. However, performance worsens in a zero-shot learning setup, with a mean error increasing to 1173.3 km.

Conclusion: The study showcases the potential of continuous geographic modeling for dialect geolocation and corroborates the Arabic dialect continuum hypothesis, though it identifies areas requiring significant improvement.

Abstract: We present a regression-based approach to Arabic dialect geolocation that models dialectal variation as a continuous geographic space rather than discrete categories. Speaker origin is predicted as continuous latitude-longitude coordinates using a hierarchical neural architecture that fuses frame-level XLS-R-300M and Whisper-large-v3 encoder representations with phonotactic descriptors through a Transformer encoder and a learnable attention-pooled query. A spherical geodesic loss directly optimizes great-circle distance on Earth's surface, avoiding distortions inherent to planar coordinate regression. Under a leakage-free 5-fold GroupKFold protocol grouped by source recording, our model attains a pooled median localization error of 481.2 km. Auxiliary country and city heads reach 64.5% and 45.2% accuracy, respectively. A permutation Mantel test on the learned latent space provides quantitative support for the Arabic dialect continuum hypothesis. To probe true generalization, we further introduce a city-masking protocol in which two cities per fold are removed from training but retained in validation. Under this zero-shot regime, the mean error rises to 1173.3 km, a 1.32x degradation relative to seen cities. Our findings establish continuous geographic modeling as a principled framework for Arabic dialect geolocation and quantify both its strengths and the substantial headroom that remains.

</details>


### [63] [When Reasoning Narrows the Move: Diversity Collapse in LLM Game Play](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19523)
*Junyi Sha,Renfei Tan,David Simchi-Levi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) can reduce action diversity in models trained for sequential decision-making tasks, often leading to premature loss of exploratory behaviors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how supervised fine-tuning impacts behavioral diversity in decision-making tasks and explore methods to mitigate diversity collapse.

Method: Investigated effects of SFT through deterministic board game simulations, measured diversity and accuracy at various stages, and evaluated action augmentation techniques.

Result: SFT suppresses action diversity while improving accuracy, often causing premature diversity collapse. Action augmentation mitigates some of this collapse.

Conclusion: Preserving action diversity during fine-tuning is crucial for maintaining exploratory decision-making behaviors in large language models.

Abstract: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is widely used to adapt large language models to downstream tasks, but its effect on behavioral diversity in sequential decision-making remains under-explored. We study this question in a controlled suite of deterministic board games based on tic-tac-toe variants, where optimal actions are exactly computable and diversity can be measured directly. Across state-level evaluation, arena gameplay, and training trajectories, we find that reasoning-mode generation frequently suppresses action diversity without uniformly improving action accuracy. Furthermore, standard SFT improves accuracy but often induces premature diversity collapse, which exceeds what is minimally required by the accuracy-diversity tradeoff. We then show that action augmentation, which trains on all optimal actions per state rather than a single demonstrated action, would partially mitigates this effect. Our results identify narrow-support imitation as a source of policy collapse in LLM decision-making and suggest that preserving action support during SFT is important for maintaining exploratory behavior.

</details>


### [64] [On the Computational Complexity of Structural Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19573)
*Zichao Wei*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper defines structural generalization and explores its mathematical underpinnings, focusing on the limitations of pure Transformers in achieving it autonomously.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to formally define structural generalization as it has been repeatedly measured in NLP benchmarks but lacks a concrete definition. The study aims to clarify the critical gap between learned and given capacities in Transformer models.

Method: The authors propose a mathematical definition for structural generalization based on compositional structure and unbounded generalization. They analyze pure Transformers' learnability ceiling against computational complexity bounds, involving concepts like NC^1, TC^0, and neuro-symbolic systems.

Result: The result demonstrates that pure Transformers cannot autonomously learn structural generalization due to the learnability restriction of TC^0 ≠ NC^1 under standard assumptions. Neuro-symbolic systems, however, perform better by incorporating the semantic projection Gₓ.

Conclusion: The study concludes that current benchmark scores are insufficient to assess if structural generalization is genuinely learned or externally injected, highlighting a critical limitation in evaluating Transformer-based systems.

Abstract: Structural generalization has been measured repeatedly by several benchmarks, yet it has never been formally defined. We give a definition that translates the two premises (compositional structure and unbounded generalization) into mathematical language. The definition itself is neutral: a compiler that hard-codes the rules satisfies it just as well. But structural generalization becomes a scientific question only insofar as the capacity can autonomously emerge from finite data. This question pits the computational lower bound $\mathrm{NC}^1$ against the learnable ceiling $\mathrm{TC}^0$ of pure Transformers. Under a Montagovian instantiation, each compositional rule splits into two projections: a syntactic face ($F_γ$) and a semantic face ($G_γ$). Tree evaluation on the $G_γ$ side is an instantiation of BFVP, which is $\mathrm{NC}^1$-complete (Buss, 1987). A pure Transformer must learn both faces at once, but Kraus et al. (2026) prove that its learnable class $\subseteq \mathrm{TC}^0$. Under the standard assumption $\mathrm{TC}^0 \neq \mathrm{NC}^1$, a pure Transformer cannot learn structural generalization. Neuro-symbolic systems achieve the best benchmark scores precisely because they inject $G_γ$, sidestepping the genuinely hard half. Benchmark scores cannot distinguish "learned" from "given." This is what this paper sets out to make clear.

</details>


### [65] [Scaling Laws for Hypernetwork-Based Knowledge Injection in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19604)
*Nischay Dhankhar,Dos Baha,Abulhair Saparov*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates using hypernetworks for injecting factual knowledge into large language models during training, finding scalable and predictive benefits compared to alternative methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in reliably injecting factual knowledge into large language models at scale and study the scaling behaviors of hypernetwork architectures in this context.

Method: The authors use hypernetworks to generate fixed LoRA adapters for train-time knowledge injection and analyze scaling laws through experiments involving architecture variations and a large dataset, MegaWikiQA.

Result: Hypernetworks demonstrate predictive scaling power and reliable out-of-distribution generalization, outperforming LoRA finetuning and full fine-tuning in scaling metrics.

Conclusion: Hypernetworks provide a scalable and effective substrate for factual knowledge injection and train-time adaptation, backed by the first empirical scaling laws for this architecture.

Abstract: Injecting factual knowledge into large language models (LLMs) reliably and at scale remains an open challenge. Hypernetworks provide a promising solution to large-scale knowledge injection. Although hypernetworks are typically applied for test-time adaptation, we explore their use in train-time knowledge injection, where, given a large corpus of facts, we train a hypernetwork to generate a fixed LoRA adapter that, when inserted into the target model, enable the model to answer questions about those facts. In this work, we investigate whether hypernetworks can be used to perform train-time knowledge injection and how this ability varies with scale. The scaling behavior of hypernetworks remains largely unstudied. Our design decouples the hypernetwork's injection capacity from the target model's general capability, enabling, for the first time, a rigorous study of scaling laws for hypernetwork architectures. We characterize how loss, reasoning accuracy, and out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization vary with hypernetwork depth, width, and target network size. We construct a large-scale dataset, called MegaWikiQA, containing tens of millions of multi-hop question-answer examples across 39 domains constructed from examples in Wikidata5M. Our results reveal: (i) hypernetwork-based injection exhibits broadly predictive power law scaling along all architecture axes; and (ii) hypernetworks are capable of reliable OOD generalization at increasing scales, suggesting that hypernetwork provides a promising alternative to other train-time adaptation methods such as LoRA finetuning and full fine-tuning, exhibiting steeper scaling exponents in all OOD evaluations. Together, these results establish hypernetworks as a principled and scalable substrate for train-time adaptation, and provide the first empirically grounded scaling laws to guide hypernetworks for factual reasoning in large language models.

</details>


### [66] [Task Competence Is Not Instruction Following: Evaluating Instruction-Conflicting Behavior in Small Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19608)
*Mahdiyeh Farajidizaji,Vatsal Raina*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper studies whether small language models adhere to conflicting instructions against their usual task behavior. It examines multiple tasks and introduces metrics like Instruction-Following Failure Rate (IFFR). It concludes that ability to follow instructions is distinct from standard accuracy and scales with model size.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper seeks to understand if small instruction-tuned language models comply with conflicting instructions and to investigate whether task characteristics or broader tendencies affect adherence.

Method: The authors evaluate models using conflicting standard and non-standard instructions across three distinct tasks, utilizing metrics like standard accuracy, non-standard accuracy, and IFFR.

Result: Results show that larger models tend to balance instruction following and task competence better than smaller ones, which often ignore conflicting instructions to preserve accuracy.

Conclusion: Instruction-following ability differs from task competence, with larger models being more adept at adhering to conflicting instructions, highlighting that reporting only standard accuracy is insufficient to understand model behavior.

Abstract: Instruction tuning is meant to make language models follow user requests, yet it is unclear whether small models comply when an instruction conflicts with their usual task behavior. We study this across three tasks - multiple-choice question answering (MCQA), sentiment classification, and mathematical question answering - by pairing a standard instruction with a conflicting non-standard one (select an incorrect option, output the opposite sentiment, or return twice the answer). This cross-task design allows us to test whether resistance to conflicting instructions is tied to specific task characteristics or reflects a broader behavioral tendency. As all predictions are scored against the original ground truth, a model that ignores the non-standard instruction still appears accurate. Using standard accuracy, non-standard accuracy, and an Instruction-Following Failure Rate (IFFR), we evaluate instruction-tuned Qwen models across sizes. Both standard accuracy and instruction following generally improve with scale, although the pattern is not consistent across all tasks and datasets. Small models stay competent yet routinely ignore the non-standard instruction, while larger models show a clear gap between the two settings. These findings suggest that gains in task capability do not automatically provide reliable control over model behavior. Task competence and instruction following are therefore distinct abilities, and reporting only standard accuracy hides instruction-following failures.

</details>


### [67] [Adaptive Capitulation: A Structural Failure Mode of LLM Responses in Vulnerability Contexts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19629)
*Eunna Lee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Large language models (LLMs) struggle to balance protective restriction, uninfluenced facilitation, and a mix of both when dealing with emotionally sensitive user queries. The study identifies a failure mode, adaptive capitulation, and proposes Minimal Reattributive Sufficiency (MRS) as a solution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of designing LLMs capable of managing emotionally sensitive interactions without reinforcing maladaptive tendencies in vulnerable users.

Method: The authors tested three commercial LLMs in 900 escalating vulnerability scenarios, coding responses with two binary indices: VCC (Validation of the Context's Claim) and VCI (Validation of the Context's Implication).

Result: The study revealed a failure mode called adaptive capitulation, where LLMs validate distress-driven maladaptive goals before facilitating them. It also confirmed the inherent structural trilemma in current system designs.

Conclusion: Minimal Reattributive Sufficiency (MRS) is introduced as a design principle, embedding subtle cues to support autonomous reattribution within validating responses.

Abstract: Large language models operating in emotionally sensitive contexts face a structural trilemma: when users in vulnerable states request information that may reinforce maladaptive attribution, current response architectures resolve the tension through protective restriction, uninflected facilitation, or unintegrated co-presence of both imperatives -- each preserving one objective at the cost of the other. Administering a three-turn escalating vulnerability vignette to three commercial LLMs (900 sessions across material, relational, and somatic status-proxy variants) and coding responses with two binary indices (VCC/VCI), we characterize a previously undocumented failure mode we term adaptive capitulation: the model validates the social injustice underlying the user's distress before pivoting to detailed facilitation of the very acquisition it nominally discouraged. We show that the trilemma is structural rather than incidental, and propose Minimal Reattributive Sufficiency (MRS), an architecture-neutral design principle that embeds a single reattributive cue within an otherwise validating response, preserving a pathway toward autonomous reattribution without contesting the user's stated goal.

</details>


### [68] [Reference-Free Evaluation of Reasoning in Open-Ended Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19678)
*Guneet Singh Kohli,Yuxiang Zhou,Michael Sejr Schlichtkrull,Gregory E Dean,Maria Liakata*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a reasoning-based audit method for verifying multi-step responses from LLMs, using inference hypergraphs and backtracking search to evaluate reasoning quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-stakes domains require reliable methods to verify multi-step reasoning in LLM outputs, which are often fluent but lack proper grounding.

Method: The approach involves decomposing reasoning traces into segments, labeling premise-target relations via NLI, constructing hypergraphs, and performing a deterministic backward search for audit labeling.

Result: The proposed framework outperforms LLM-as-judge baselines in accurately identifying problematic reasoning segments during mathematical and medical evaluations.

Conclusion: The study highlights the need for reasoning trace-level evaluation in QA systems, offering more reliable auditing methods for LLM-generated reasoning, along with UroReason and open-source code availability.

Abstract: AI-generated answers in high-stakes domains are often fluent but difficult to verify, especially when they contain multi-step reasoning rather than a single final answer. We propose a reasoning-based, reference-free framework for auditing LLM-generated outputs. The method decomposes a generated reasoning trace into segments, labels local premise-target relations using Natural Language Inference (NLI), and organizes these relations into a hypergraph. A deterministic backward AND-OR search then assigns segment-level audit labels that indicate how each segment is grounded within the generated response. We evaluate the framework in two settings: deductive mathematical reasoning with Hard2Verify, and open-ended medical reasoning with UroReason, a new physician-annotated benchmark of LLM reasoning traces from real clinical cases. Across these settings, our NLI-hypergraph audit provides a more reliable reference-free evaluation signal than direct LLM-as-judge baselines. In the clinical setting, state-of-the-art LLM judges often fail to identify problematic reasoning segments, over-accepting fluent but weakly grounded responses. Our results show that QA evaluation should account for how inferential relations compose across a reasoning trace, rather than relying only on final answers or LLMs as verifiers. UroReason will be made available through an API, and our code will be released as open source.

</details>


### [69] [Multi-Mask Diffusion Language Models for Few-Step Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19686)
*Sijin Chen,Yinuo Ren,Heyang Zhao,Ziheng Cheng,Quanquan Gu,Lexing Ying*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces multi-mask diffusion models (MultiMDM) to improve few-step language generation, addressing issues in previous models like MDMs and uniform-state diffusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome challenges in few-step generation with Masked Diffusion Models (MDMs), such as terminal entropy collapse to a single masked state, and to improve generation quality and training efficiency.

Method: Proposed a MultiMDM approach that retains the masking structure in few-step generation. The forward process introduces a designated mask before gradually mixing. A closed-form ELBO training objective was developed, and a discrete-state consistency distillation method was designed with shared-Gumbel coupling.

Result: MultiMDM achieves effective few-step generation and can leverage pretrained MDMs. Experiments confirm its efficacy in both pretraining and distillation.

Conclusion: The methodological enhancements in MultiMDM make it a strong and principled framework for high-quality few-step language generation.

Abstract: Masked diffusion models (MDMs) are a promising family of language generators, but achieving high-quality few-step generation remains challenging. In MDMs, all forward trajectories collapse to a single fully masked state, leaving no terminal entropy for consistency-style few-step generation. While recent few-step alternatives based on uniform-state diffusion avoid this degeneracy, it becomes harder to distinguish clean tokens from noise than MDMs, which usually harms modeling quality and training efficiency. In this work, we propose a multi-mask diffusion model (MultiMDM) that preserves the masking structure towards few-step generation. In the forward process, each clean token is first pushed towards a designated mask and then gradually mixes over the mask set. As a result, the backward process has a drafting capability by predicting a designated mask before refining to a clean token. We derive a closed-form ELBO training objective for MultiMDM that supports continual training from pretrained MDMs. In addition, we formulate a purely discrete-state consistency distillation scheme, with a shared-Gumbel coupling to reduce pathwise entropy. Experiments on pretraining and distillation show that MultiMDM provides an effective foundation for principled few-step generation.

</details>


### [70] [SLPO: Scaling Latent Reasoning via a Surrogate Policy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19691)
*Runyang You,Zhiyuan Liu,Yongqi Li,Wenjie Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces "Surrogate Latent Policy Optimization (SLPO)", which enables Reinforcement Learning (RL) with outcome rewards for latent reasoning systems, enhancing test-time scaling and performance in both continuous and soft thinking tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address limitations of explicit Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning methods, which are computationally expensive during test-time scaling, by exploring latent reasoning as a more efficient alternative. Existing latent reasoners are restricted to imitation-based learning, limiting their scalability.

Method: SLPO incorporates an empirical surrogate policy density for trajectory-level credit assignment and introduces a correctness-supervised stopping mechanism, optimizing variable-horizon policies for latent reasoning systems.

Result: SLPO improves performance metrics like Pass@$k$, allows for more computational allocation to complex tasks, and achieves higher deterministic accuracy, demonstrating enhanced reasoning efficiency.

Conclusion: SLPO successfully brings outcome-reward RL to latent reasoners, bridging gaps in intermediate computation handling and adaptive stopping, and unlocking better scalability and reasoning capabilities.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has become the predominant recipe for eliciting test-time scaling in explicit Chain-of-Thought reasoners. Yet this scaling path remains computationally costly, since every intermediate step must be decoded as a language token. Latent reasoning instead carries intermediate computation as continuous vectors and already matches or surpasses explicit CoT at far shorter horizons. Despite this promise, latent reasoners remain largely imitation-bound, while explicit CoT has already moved past imitation via outcome-reward RL. Latent trajectories lack a tractable per-step likelihood and an adaptive stopping interface under fixed thinking budgets, so outcome rewards cannot elicit latent test-time scaling. We introduce Surrogate Latent Policy Optimization (SLPO) to bring outcome-reward RL to autoregressive latent reasoners: an empirical surrogate policy density over latent transitions for trajectory-level credit assignment, and a correctness-supervised stopping head that outcome-reward optimization refines into a variable-horizon policy. Across continuous and soft thinking settings, SLPO improves Pass@$k$ under parallel sampling and allocates longer latent computation to harder instances with higher deterministic accuracy.

</details>


### [71] [Lightweight Person-Place Relation Extraction from Historical Newspapers with Dependency Graphs and Proximity Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19718)
*Mlen-Too Wesley*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents a lightweight, interpretable system for person-place relation extraction in historical multilingual newspapers, achieving notable recall efficiency without reliance on pretrained language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the challenge of processing historical archives at scale in a resource-efficient manner without using costly pretrained language models.

Method: The proposed system builds document-level graphs from dependency parses, extracts proximity and part-of-speech features, and classifies relations using small scikit-learn ensembles or compact Graph Attention Networks with low computational requirements.

Result: The best run achieved a macro recall of 0.5142, ranking 3rd on Efficiency and mid-table on Accuracy among 17 teams, highlighting the influence of minimum character distance and the importance of document-grouped cross-validation.

Conclusion: Lightweight systems using engineered features can effectively handle relation extraction when guided by efficient models and robust validation techniques, though simpler distance metrics may suffice for signal capture.

Abstract: The HIPE-2026 shared task introduces person-place relation extraction from multilingual historical newspapers as a new evaluation track, classifying the at and isAt relations between pre-annotated person and location mentions in English, French, and German. Motivated by the cost of processing historical archives at scale, our team (DS@GT HIPE, team 2 in the official results) investigates how far a lightweight, interpretable system can go without any pretrained language model at the relation classification stage. Our approach builds a document-level graph from dependency parses, extracts proximity-based and part-of-speech features for each entity pair, and classifies them with small scikit-learn ensembles or compact Graph Attention Networks, keeping every submitted run under 847K parameters. On the official evaluation (Test A, the newspaper test set), our best run reached a macro recall of 0.5142, ranking 3rd on the Efficiency profile while placing mid-table on Accuracy among the 17 participating teams. Two findings stand out. First, minimum character distance alone captures most of the classification signal; adding further engineered features yields inconsistent gains and sometimes degrades performance, echoing prior evidence that argument distance dominates relation extraction. Second, document-grouped cross-validation is essential on this corpus: pair-level splits inflate scores by 25-37 percentage points because entity mentions recur across documents, a data-leakage effect that grouped cross-validation removes.

</details>


### [72] [Beyond Relevance-Centric Retrieval: Rubric-Oriented Document Set Selection and Ranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19747)
*Kailin Jiang,Lei Liu,Jian Xi,Hui Xu,Junlin Liu,Baochen Fu,Shaoqing Ren,Bin Li,Vichwang,Yu Lu,Haibo Shi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces SetwiseEvalKit to evaluate document sets comprehensively in terms of quality and inter-document interactions, crucial for large language models' downstream performance. It also proposes Rubric4Setwise for optimizing document set selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation systems for document sets fail to address inter-document interactions (e.g., redundancy, conflict, complementarity) and cannot effectively compare the overall quality of document sets, which impacts the downstream generation by large language models.

Method: The authors designed SetwiseEvalKit, a benchmark with three levels and nine dimensions covering both short- and long-form evaluation scenarios. They evaluated 12 methods and introduced Rubric4Setwise, a training-free approach using rubric-based criteria for document selection.

Result: SetwiseEvalKit exposed weaknesses in all tested rerankers, with the best achieving only 45% coverage and inconsistent performance across scenarios. Rubric4Setwise emerged as the best, maintaining state-of-the-art downstream generation performance and reducing the number of documents and search rounds required.

Conclusion: Rubric4Setwise effectively bridges evaluation and optimization, outperforming existing methods and highlighting the importance of holistic evaluation frameworks like SetwiseEvalKit in improving document set quality for AI consumption.

Abstract: As large language models and AI agents become the primary consumers of search results, document set quality determines the upper bound of downstream generation. Yet existing evaluation systems remain confined to scoring documents independently and aggregating via nDCG, ignoring inter-document interactions (redundancy, conflict, complementarity) and unable to answer what makes one document set better than another. To address these issues, we propose a complete evaluate-diagnose-optimize framework. We design SetwiseEvalKit, a three-level, nine-dimension document set evaluation benchmark covering both short-form and long-form scenarios, comprising approximately 28K high-quality evaluation rubrics. We systematically evaluate 12 rerankers: even the best method achieves no more than 45% coverage, cross-document coordination dimensions are universally weak, and no single method maintains top performance across both settings. Building on this, we propose Rubric4Setwise, a training-free method that converts rubric-based evaluation criteria into document set selection signals, achieving the best downstream generation performance with fewer documents and search rounds. It is the only method that maintains state-of-the-art results across both scenarios, validating the effectiveness of closing the loop from evaluation to optimization.

</details>


### [73] [TriAgent: Divergence-Aware Multi-Agent Committees for Cost-Efficient Financial Sentiment Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19794)
*Isabel Xu,Cynthia Xu,Rachel Ren,Cong Guo,Jiacheng Ding*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces TriAgent, a cost-efficient, multi-agent framework for financial sentiment analysis using stratified contextual granularity and a Semantic Divergence Index (SDI) to achieve high performance while minimizing costs in large-scale systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the structural cost inefficiency in Large Language Model (LLM)-based financial sentiment analysis, where most queries are simple but are processed by expensive models, leading to high operational costs that scale linearly with the number of users.

Method: TriAgent employs a multi-agent committee consisting of a lexicon-based analyzer (VADER), a domain-specific transformer (FinBERT), and a cross-sentence LLM reasoner (Qwen2.5 and other models with cross-family checks), paired with an SDI to detect disagreement among agents and route queries effectively.

Result: The study finds that re-tasking an LLM as a critic plateaus its F1 performance (~0.87) regardless of scale. The SDI enables a high accuracy Shared Consensus Dictionary for cross-lingual queries, hallucination detection, and superior financial back-testing results. At scale, TriAgent outperforms fixed-model strategies like always-FinBERT or always-LLM approaches while drastically reducing costs.

Conclusion: TriAgent offers a cost-effective, performance-optimized solution for financial sentiment analysis, leveraging the complementary strengths of a multi-agent system. Its design principles, driven by SDI and contextual granularity, provide practical solutions to scalability, accuracy, and budgeting challenges in production deployment.

Abstract: Production LLM-based financial sentiment analysis faces a structural cost trap: most queries are trivially classifiable, yet expensive cloud reasoners process them all, and the bill scales linearly with user count. We present TriAgent, a multi-agent committee stratified by contextual granularity -- a word-level lexicon (VADER), a sentence-level domain transformer (FinBERT), and a cross-sentence reasoner (Qwen2.5, 0.5B-14B-4bit, with Mistral-7B and Phi-3.5-mini cross-family checks). A three-way Semantic Divergence Index (SDI) measures pairwise disagreement across granularities and routes each query accordingly. Our central finding is the critic plateau: when the LLM is re-tasked as a critic over the smaller agents' outputs, F1 plateaus at ~0.87 across 1.5B-7B Qwen (bootstrap 95% CIs overlap), while a same-size 3-persona vote drops to F1=0.66, which is driven by granularity-stratified diversity. Three corollaries follow from the same SDI signal: (i) a Shared Consensus Dictionary on multilingual sentence-BERT answers 95% of Chinese queries from an English cache at F1=0.99 -- cross-border canonicalization at zero marginal cost; (ii) SDI doubles as a post-hoc LLM-hallucination detector at AUC=0.90; (iii) the SDI single-stage strategy attains the best risk-adjusted return (Sharpe=3.50) on a 20-ticker back-test, dominating both always-FinBERT (1.36) and always-LLM (0.11). At 10M-user scale, TriAgent saves $9.3M/year vs. a GPT-4o-mini baseline. Code, lexicons, and the SCD are released.

</details>


### [74] [VizRAG: Enhancing Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Hypergraph Visualization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19830)
*Yanbin Wei,Yang Chen,Renling Gan,Ziru Liu,Xinyu Fu,Chun Kang,Ning Lu,Rui Liu,Yu Zhang,James Kwok*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces VizRAG, a hypergraph-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system that incorporates visual representations of hypergraphs, showing improved performance over baseline methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current hypergraph-based RAG systems are text-centric and fail to utilize the potential of enhanced visual perception capabilities in modern multimodal large language models.

Method: The paper proposes integrating visual representations of hypergraphs into the RAG pipeline, thereby enabling hypergraph-aware information retrieval and reconstruction.

Result: Experiments show that VizRAG outperforms existing strong baseline RAG systems, highlighting the benefits of visual hypergraph incorporation.

Conclusion: Incorporating visual hypergraph awareness into RAG systems can unlock significant performance improvements, showcasing a new direction in the field.

Abstract: Hypergraph-based RAG systems surpass traditional graph-based approaches by organizing complex n-ary atomic facts among entities, rather than relying solely on binary relationships. Despite the advancements in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) with enhanced visual capabilities, current hypergraph-based RAG frameworks predominantly restrict knowledge retrieval and reconstruction to a unimodal, text-centric paradigm. This limitation prevents them from fully leveraging the powerful visual perception capabilities of modern MLLMs. To address this gap, we systematically explore the integration of hypergraph awareness in RAG systems through visual cues. By incorporating visual representations of hypergraphs into the RAG pipeline, we introduce VizRAG, the first RAG system to support visual hypergraph structure awareness. Experimental results demonstrate that VizRAG significantly outperforms strong baselines, validating the promising potential of hypergraph visualization as a novel approach for RAG systems.

</details>


### [75] [D2VBench: Benchmarking Large Language Models with Value Dilemmas in Daily Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19834)
*Siyi Hao,Yidi Cao,Linhao Yu,Yuqi Ren,Deyi Xiong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: D2VBench introduces a benchmark of 10,000 dilemmas focusing on value alignment for LLMs, incorporating human and AI collaboration for fine-grained evaluation methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation benchmarks for LLMs fail to adequately address complex value dilemmas and their alignment with real-world scenarios.

Method: The authors developed a dataset consisting of 10,000 dilemmas based on 158 fine-grained value concepts through collaboration between humans and LLMs and introduced a hybrid evaluation method using multiple-choice and open-ended questions.

Result: D2VBench provides high reliability and robustness in evaluating LLMs' value alignment capabilities across diverse categories and dimensions.

Conclusion: D2VBench is a significant and realistic tool for advancing research on value alignment in LLMs, offering a granular evaluation framework.

Abstract: With the wide application of large language models (LLMs) in real-world scenarios, the value implication of their outputs is crucial. However, existing evaluation benchmarks suffer from insufficient coverage of value dilemmas in daily scenarios involving multiple value conflicts and simplistic evaluation formalisms that fail to assess LLMs' value alignment. To address these issues, we propose D2VBench, a value alignment benchmark comprising 10,000 instances of real daily dilemma scenarios constructed through a multi-stage collaboration between LLMs and humans, grounded in 158 manually annotated fine-grained value concepts. For evaluation on the benchmark, we present a hybrid evaluation paradigm that integrates multiple-choice questions with open-ended questions. We conduct comprehensive evaluations on eight mainstream LLMs. Experimental results demonstrate that D2VBench exhibits high reliability and robustness, effectively reflecting the LLMs' alignment across different value categories and dimensions, and providing a more realistic and fine-grained tool for research on value alignment. The dataset is available at https://github.com/tjunlp-lab/D2VBench.

</details>


### [76] [Sentence Splitter: Uncovering Latent Factual Structure for Self-Supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19845)
*Ahmad Pouramini,Mahsa Afsharizadeh*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces "Sentence Splitter," a T5-based framework for identifying semantic boundaries in sentences, and demonstrates its effectiveness in improving performance on knowledge-driven NLP tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of uncovering latent factual structures in natural language sentences to improve knowledge-centric NLP tasks, without relying on manual annotations.

Method: The authors propose a self-supervised, T5-based encoder-decoder model that formulates sentence segmentation as a discrete optimization problem. It first verbalizes symbolic head-tail pairs into natural language templates for model training, then applies the trained model to raw text, eventually bootstrapping data through probabilistic generation of plausible completions.

Result: Experiments confirm the generalizability of the proposed model beyond synthetic templates and its structure-aware supervision improves performance on tasks like knowledge graph completion and commonsense question answering.

Conclusion: Sentence Splitter effectively bridges symbolic knowledge and natural language, demonstrating scalability and structure-awareness while enhancing performance on knowledge-centric tasks.

Abstract: This paper introduces Sentence Splitter, a self-supervised framework built upon a T5-based encoder--decoder architecture for uncovering the latent factual structure of natural language sentences. The proposed method identifies the semantic boundary between a descriptive prefix (head) and its factual completion (tail) by formulating sentence splitting as a discrete segmentation problem, where a sentence of length $N$ admits $N$ possible split points but only one recovers the intended head--tail structure. Rather than explicitly searching over all candidate boundaries, the model learns to recover the factual completion through probabilistic sequence generation. To eliminate the need for manual annotation, symbolic head--tail pairs are first verbalized into natural-language templates that provide supervision for training the Sentence Splitter. The trained splitter is then applied to raw text to extract aligned prefix--tail pairs, which are subsequently used to train a generative model that proposes additional plausible completions through a lightweight bootstrapping process. This unified pipeline provides a scalable and structure-aware approach to constructing self-supervised training data while bridging symbolic knowledge and natural language. Experiments on both structured and naturally occurring text demonstrate that the proposed splitter generalizes beyond synthetic templates and that the resulting structure-aware supervision consistently improves downstream performance on knowledge graph completion and commonsense question answering, highlighting the effectiveness of recovering latent factual structure for knowledge-centric NLP.

</details>


### [77] [emb-diversity: A Tool for Embedding-Based Measurement of Data Diversity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19848)
*Cantao Su,Menan Velayuthan,Esther Ploeger,Dong Nguyen,Anna Wegmann*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces "emb-diversity," a tool for measuring diversity in datasets using embeddings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods of measuring diversity in NLP datasets are inconsistent and lack standardization, especially regarding embedding-based measures.

Method: The authors developed emb-diversity, a tool leveraging embeddings to quantify various forms of diversity in datasets, such as stylistic, semantic, language, and speaker diversity.

Result: emb-diversity offers a flexible framework that is compatible with any embedding model and dataset, enabling robust measurements across multiple dimensions of diversity.

Conclusion: Embedding-based diversity measurements, as demonstrated by emb-diversity, can improve the robustness and fairness of NLP models by offering standardized and comprehensive diversity evaluation tools.

Abstract: There is growing evidence that data diversity is crucial for developing fair and robust NLP models. However, current approaches to measure diversity remain inconsistent and fragmented: While there exist a number of tools for measuring the lexical diversity of texts, researchers lack standardized tools for quantifying diversity based on embeddings. Embedding-based diversity measures are highly flexible: They work with any embedding model and any data that can be embedded, and are thus applicable to many notions of diversity. With emb-diversity, we provide a comprehensive embedding-based diversity measurement tool, spanning a broad range of measures. We demonstrate its potential for several use cases: measuring the stylistic, semantic, language and speaker diversity of datasets. https://github.com/nlpsoc/emb-diversity/

</details>


### [78] [Overview of FinMMEval 2026 Task 1: Multilingual Financial Multiple-Choice Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19856)
*Zhuohan Xie,Yuyang Dai,Rania Elbadry,Vanshikaa Jani,Georgi Georgiev,Dimitar Dimitrov,Fan Zhang,Xueqing Peng,Lingfei Qian,Jimin Huang,Jiahui Geng,Yankai Chen,Ye Yuan,Haolun Wu,Yuxia Wang,Ivan Koychev,Veselin Stoyanov,Mingzi Song,Yu Chen,Xue Liu,Preslav Nakov*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: FinMMEval 2026 Task 1 evaluates multilingual financial multiple-choice question answering across four languages (English, Chinese, Arabic, and Hindi), achieving high accuracy scores.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess the ability of systems to handle multilingual financial question answering, incorporating domain-specific knowledge, numerical reasoning, and conceptual understanding.

Method: Created a multilingual test set of 800 questions across four languages, ranked submissions by accuracy, and documented methodologies such as retrieval augmentation, language-specific prompting, and LLM-based review.

Result: Top accuracy scores ranged from 92.0% (Hindi) to 97.5% (English and Arabic), with leading teams performing consistently well across all languages.

Conclusion: The task demonstrates substantial progress in multilingual financial QA with modern techniques, though differences in accuracy highlight varying system capabilities across languages.

Abstract: FinMMEval 2026 Task 1 evaluates multilingual financial multiple-choice question answering in English, Chinese, Arabic, and Hindi. The task tests whether systems can select the correct answer to finance questions involving domain terminology, numerical interpretation, and conceptual financial reasoning across languages and scripts. The final-test set contains 800 questions, with 200 questions per language; gold answers were withheld during submission, and each language was ranked independently by accuracy. The final leaderboards contain 13 English, 11 Chinese, 11 Arabic, and 10 Hindi ranked submissions. Top accuracies range from 92.0% in Hindi to 97.5% in English and Arabic, with the same leading teams appearing near the top across all four languages. The documented systems used retrieval augmentation, direct answer-option scoring, language-specific prompting, selective self-consistency, confidence checks, and LLM-based review stages.

</details>


### [79] [Overview of FinMMEval 2026 Task 2: Multilingual Financial Short-Answer Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19867)
*Zhuohan Xie,Xueqing Peng,Georgi Georgiev,Dimitar Dimitrov,Yuyang Dai,Rania Elbadry,Vanshikaa Jani,Lingfei Qian,Fan Zhang,Jimin Huang,Jiahui Geng,Yankai Chen,Ye Yuan,Haolun Wu,Yuxia Wang,Ivan Koychev,Veselin Stoyanov,Mingzi Song,Yu Chen,Xue Liu,Preslav Nakov*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: FinMMEval 2026 Task 2 assesses multilingual short-answer question answering in the financial domain, with submissions ranked using ROUGE-1 F1.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to advance the evaluation of systems that can accurately answer financial questions based on multilingual sources.

Method: Participants answered English questions using financial text in multiple languages, with answers submitted in JSONL format and evaluated via ROUGE-1 F1.

Result: 12 submissions were ranked, with the top systems achieving close competition; the top four were separated by less than 1% in ROUGE-1 F1.

Conclusion: Strategies such as retrieval-augmented generation, cross-lingual evidence handling, and answer compression were pivotal for system performance.

Abstract: FinMMEval 2026 Task 2 evaluates short-answer financial question answering over multilingual evidence. Each final-test item pairs an English question with financial statements and news in English, Chinese, Japanese, Spanish, and Greek. Participating systems submit one concise answer per item in JSONL format. The final-test set contains 256 items, split evenly between easy and expert tiers; each tier contains four question templates instantiated over 32 company-report groups. Gold answers were withheld during submission, and systems were ranked by macro-averaged item-level ROUGE-1 F1 against organizer-held reference answers. The final leaderboard includes 12 ranked submissions. The strongest systems are closely clustered, with the top four separated by less than one percentage point in ROUGE-1 F1. The submitted system papers document retrieval-augmented generation, cross-lingual evidence handling, structured prompting, answer compression, and validation strategies.

</details>


### [80] [Efficient Chain-of-Modality Reasoning via Progressive Compression for Spoken Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19932)
*Pengchao Feng,Chao-Hong Tan,Qian Chen,Wen Wang,Xiangang Li,Xie Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces "Efficient Chain-of-Modality Reasoning" (ECoM Reasoning), a framework aimed at improving spoken language models (SLMs) on spoken mathematical question-answering tasks by compressing the reasoning process and reducing token usage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the gap in reasoning abilities between spoken language models and text-based large language models, especially for solving spoken mathematical question-answering, due to the difficulty of reasoning over purely verbalized mathematical expressions.

Method: The proposed ECoM Reasoning framework introduces compressed reasoning to reduce token usage while maintaining accuracy. It employs "Progressive Compression," a curriculum-based training strategy moving from full-form reasoning to compressed reasoning.

Result: ECoM Reasoning improves reasoning accuracy by 21% over standard Chain-of-Modality (CoM) architectures without reasoning and by 3% over CoM with full reasoning traces, all while using only 40% of the text tokens.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that compressed reasoning via ECoM not only bridges the reasoning gap for SLMs but also achieves better inference efficiency, making it an effective approach for enhancing spoken mathematical question answering.

Abstract: Spoken language models (SLMs) enable natural human-computer interaction, but their reasoning ability still lags behind that of text-based large language models, especially on spoken mathematical question answering tasks. One important reason is that SLMs reason over purely verbalized mathematical expressions, which are harder to interpret than symbolic text. However, directly transferring text-based reasoning to SLMs is nontrivial due to architectural constraints and the additional computational requirements. To address this challenge, we propose Efficient Chain-of-Modality Reasoning (ECoM Reasoning), the first framework to introduce compressed reasoning into SLMs. By compressing the textual component so that it jointly serves as speech guidance and reasoning representation, ECoM Reasoning improves reasoning accuracy while using a smaller token budget than the standard Chain-of-Modality (CoM) architecture, which generates intermediate text before speech. To train this capability, we further propose Progressive Compression, a curriculum-based strategy that gradually trains the model from full-form reasoning to compressed reasoning. Experiments on spoken mathematical question answering benchmarks show that ECoM Reasoning improves accuracy by 21% over standard CoM without explicit reasoning, and by 3% over CoM with full reasoning traces while using only 40% of the text tokens, demonstrating that it enhances SLM reasoning while remaining inference-efficient.

</details>


### [81] [A Multi-Dimensional Evaluation of Explainability in Media Bias Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19954)
*Ting Chen,Raina Zhang,Benjamin M. Ampel,Sagar Samtani*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study evaluates the explainability of media bias detection in BERT and RoBERTa models, focusing on three dimensions: predictive performance, explanation plausibility, and mechanistic faithfulness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of subtle biased framing in media and the need for predictive models that offer rational, interpretable explanations.

Method: The paper uses BERT and RoBERTa classifiers with the BABE dataset, exploring axes of performance, plausibility (token-level alignment with expert rationales), and faithfulness (effectiveness of attention heads under counterfactual rationale masking). Attention-supervised finetuning is added to study explanation plausibility.

Result: Attribution plausibility and mechanistic recoverability vary greatly across architectures and methodologies. Attention supervision influences attribution plausibility but is affected by architecture scale.

Conclusion: Predictive accuracy, explanatory plausibility, and mechanistic recoverability are distinct dimensions and should be independently evaluated for effective explainability in media bias detection models.

Abstract: Detecting media bias automatically is difficult because biased framing is often subtle, yet in domains such as news analysis, accurate predictions alone are insufficient without explanations that reflect the model's underlying reasoning. We present a multi-dimensional evaluation of explainability in encoder-based media bias detection using the Bias Annotations By Experts (BABE) dataset. Specifically, we study BERT and RoBERTa as classifiers (base and large variants) along three complementary axes: predictive performance, explanation plausibility (token-level alignment with expert rationales), and mechanistic faithfulness (whether compact sets of attention heads recover predictive signal under counterfactual rationale masking). To induce variation in plausibility, we additionally investigate attention-supervised finetuning, which incorporates expert rationale annotations as an auxiliary training signal. Attention supervision serves as an intervention on attribution plausibility, while the effectiveness of attribution methods varies substantially across architectures. Circuit analysis further reveals substantial variation in mechanistic recoverability across architectures, suggesting that model scale alone does not determine circuit compressibility. Taken together, our findings suggest that predictive performance, attribution plausibility, and mechanistic faithfulness characterize different aspects of model behavior and should be evaluated separately when studying explainability in media bias detection.

</details>


### [82] [When Does Knowledge Distillation Hurt? Reliability-Aware Distillation for Low-Resource Language Summarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19956)
*Dipto Sumit,Ankan Kumar Roy Srizon,Sadia Khair Rodela,Atia Haque Asha,Mourchona Afrin,Niloy Farhan,Farig Sadeque*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces reliability-aware knowledge distillation techniques, CHAD and EWAD+CPDP, which outperform standard KD in sequence-to-sequence model summarization tasks and generalize well across low-resource languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard knowledge distillation often yields minimal improvements and can actively harm model performance on some samples. There is a need for more effective distillation methods that account for sample-specific reliability.

Method: Two new methods are proposed. CHAD uses gradient alignment to evaluate sample utility and trains a lightweight gate to filter samples. EWAD+CPDP combines token-level entropy-weighted distillation with a capacity-proportional geometric constraint from a second teacher model.

Result: On the BanSum dataset, CHAD improves ROUGE-L by +0.0173 and EWAD+CPDP by +0.0219 over standard KD. EWAD+CPDP generalizes well across diverse XL-Sum languages, showing consistent improvements on 10 out of 15 languages tested.

Conclusion: Selective distillation methods that assess sample reliability can significantly outperform standard KD in varied linguistic contexts. The released code and models can further advance research on distillation techniques.

Abstract: Knowledge distillation (KD) is a standard approach for compressing sequence-to-sequence models, but its per-sample effects are rarely examined. On the BanSum Bangla summarization benchmark, we find that standard KD improves ROUGE-L by only +0.0003 over a cross-entropy baseline, and that approximately 51.3% of training samples are estimated to actively harm student validation loss under standard KD. We propose two complementary reliability-aware distillation methods. CHAD (Counterfactual Harm-Aware Distillation) measures per-sample KD usefulness via gradient alignment with the validation loss direction and trains a lightweight gate that generalizes this counterfactual judgment to the full training set. EWAD+CPDP combines token-level entropy-weighted adaptive distillation with a capacity-proportional geometric constraint from a second, vocabulary-incompatible teacher. On BanSum, both methods substantially outperform standard KD: CHAD by +0.0173 ROUGE-L and EWAD+CPDP by +0.0219 ROUGE-L, where standard KD itself improves ROUGE-L by only +0.0003; despite using only 60M parameters, both outperform a fine-tuned Qwen 2.5-3B model (50x larger). We further evaluate the stronger method, EWAD+CPDP, across 15 typologically diverse XL-Sum languages organised into three sets, beating the CE-only baseline on 10/15 languages; gains are most reliable where the two teachers contribute complementary signal, and weakest where they have saturated or jointly weak target-language coverage. We release code and trained models to support reproducibility and further research on selective distillation.

</details>


### [83] [TINY_SCHILLER: A Drop-In German Drama Corpus for Small Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19992)
*Mark Schutera*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: tiny_schiller simplifies German literary language model development by providing a compact, single-file solution for Schiller's dramas.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the complexities of leveraging German literary corpora for small language model prototyping and fine-tuning.

Method: Consolidated 11 Schiller dramas into a 2.07MB file with deterministic parser engineering, enabling ease-of-use setup with tokenized splits.

Result: Provided a seamless HuggingFace integration that can load various tokenization and persona splits from a single file.

Conclusion: tiny_schiller bridges a critical gap in German literary language model research by offering an accessible and efficient tool for experimentation.

Abstract: tiny_schiller closes the small-language-model prototyping, fine-tuning, education, and research gap for German literary text, providing a single-file, drop-in counterpart to Karpathy's tiny_shakespeare. The available German literary corpora are larger and richer, but require parser engineering before a single line of training or fine-tuning code can run. tiny_schiller is a 2.07-megabyte single file of eleven public-domain Schiller dramas, sourced from DraCor's GerDraCor export (CC0) and processed by deterministic parser engineering. Character-level, GPT-2 byte-pair encoding, and cl100k_base tokenization splits, an instruction-formatted dialogue-completion split, and 89 per-character persona splits load from a single HuggingFace call. A small language model literally reaches German literary text in one line of code.

</details>


### [84] [TalentCLEF at CLEF2026: Skill and Job Title Intelligence for Human Capital Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.20009)
*Luis Gasco,Hermenegildo Fabregat,Laura García-Sardiña,Paula Estrella,Casimiro Pio Carrino,Daniel Deniz,Alvaro Rodrigo,Rabih Zbib*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The TalentCLEF Challenge 2026 will focus on advancing NLP solutions in Human Capital Management through two tasks: contextualized job-person matching and job-skill matching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve fairness, multilingual capabilities, and industry adaptability in NLP for Human Capital Management.

Method: Creating public benchmarks and specific tasks for research teams to compare methods in talent matching and skill gap detection.

Result: Providing a platform to advance NLP systems addressing talent management to meet workforce demands.

Conclusion: TalentCLEF promotes practical NLP advancements in HCM by fostering collaboration and innovation in the field.

Abstract: This paper presents the second edition of the TalentCLEF Challenge, which will run as an evaluation lab as part of CLEF 2026. The aim of TalentCLEF is to promote the development of systems and methods that use Natural Language Processing (NLP) in the field of Human Capital Management (HCM), fostering approaches that ensure fairness in results, operate across multiple languages, and adapt to diverse industries. To this end, TalentCLEF establishes public benchmarks where research teams can compare methods and share findings, moving the field toward more practical and impactful NLP solutions that effectively address the real needs of workforce management.
  This year's lab will feature two tasks designed to foster the development and evaluation of systems that support key HCM activities such as talent matching, upskilling, reskilling, and skill gap detection: (i) Task A - Contextualized Job-Person Matching, focused on retrieving and ranking suitable candidates for specific job positions using context-rich and privacy-preserving data; and (ii) Task B - Job-Skill Matching with Skill Type Classification, centered on identifying relevant skills for a given job title and classifying them by their type within the job profile.
  TalentCLEF website: https://talentclef.github.io/talentclef/

</details>


### [85] [Language-Specific versus Cross-Lingual Knowledge Graphs for Implicit Aspect Identification in Arabic: A Comparative Study of Reasoning and Adaptation Strategies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.20056)
*Lujain A. Alawwad*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper compares two approaches for Aspect-based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) in Arabic: using a native Arabic knowledge graph (KG) versus a reused English KG with multilingual embeddings. The native Arabic KG outperforms the English KG, and task-specific fine-tuning proves crucial for explicit aspect extraction in Arabic.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to advance ABSA for Arabic, focusing on recovering both explicit and implicit aspects. The authors aim to address the challenges of relying on external knowledge sources in a low-resource language context by comparing English and native Arabic KGs.

Method: The paper uses a hybrid pipeline incorporating KGs and generative extractors. It evaluates the performance of a native Arabic KG versus a cross-lingual English KG and explores adaptation strategies for a generative extractor through zero-shot prompting and task-specific fine-tuning of a large language model.

Result: The native Arabic KG achieved higher micro-F1 scores (+0.199 and +0.251) on two Arabic benchmarks compared to the English KG. Task-specific fine-tuning significantly improved micro-F1 scores for explicit aspect extraction, outperforming zero-shot prompting.

Conclusion: Building a native Arabic KG is more effective than repurposing an English KG for ABSA in Arabic. Task-specific fine-tuning in large language models is essential for handling morphologically rich languages, surpassing benefits from just scaling model size.

Abstract: Aspect-based sentiment analysis (ABSA) in Arabic must recover both explicitly stated aspects and implicit aspects that are never named in the text. Implicit identification typically relies on an auxiliary knowledge source (e.g., a knowledge graph (KG)) linking opinion cues to aspect categories, but for a lower-resource language the practitioner faces a design choice: reuse a mature English KG through multilingual embeddings, or build a smaller native Arabic KG. This paper reports a controlled comparison of the two strategies within a single hybrid pipeline, evaluated on three Arabic benchmarks (M-ABSA, SemEval-2016 Arabic, and HAAD). We further compare two adaptation strategies for the generative extractor that feeds the KG -- zero-shot prompting versus task-specific fine-tuning of an 8B-parameter large language model (LLM). The native Arabic KG (Strategy 2) outperforms the cross-lingual English KG (Strategy 1) by +0.199 micro-F1 on M-ABSA and +0.251 on SemEval-2016, gaining on both precision and recall. Task-specific fine-tuning raises explicit-extraction micro-F1 from <= 0.13 (zero-shot) to 0.66-0.76 on M-ABSA and SemEval-2016 (0.45 on the smaller HAAD), confirming that task adaptation, rather than model scale, is decisive in a morphologically rich language.

</details>


### [86] [Solar Open 2 Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.20062)
*Sungrae Park,Sanghoon Kim,Gyoungjin Gim,Jungho Cho,Hyunwoong Ko,Minbyul Jeong,Minjeong Kim,Keunwoo Choi,Chaehun Shin,Chanwoong Yoon,Dongjun Kim,Eunwon Kim,Gyungin Shin,Hyeonju Lee,Hyungkyu Kang,Inseo Song,Jisu Bae,Jiyoon Han,Jiyun Lee,Joonkee Kim,Junyeop Lee,Mikyoung Cha,Sangwon Yu,Sehwan Joo,Seokyoon Kang,Seonghoon Yang,Seung Shin,Seunghyun Lee,Seungseop Lim,Seungyoun Shin,Sukyung Lee,Taegyeong Eo,Taehwan Oh,Taewhoo Lee,Wonho Song,Wonjun Oh,Wonseok Hwang,Yunsu Kim,Yura Shim,Hwalsuk Lee,Sunghun Kim,Du-Seong Chang,Kyunghyun Cho,Seungju Han,Yejin Choi,Junsuk Choe,Hwaran Lee,Minjeong Ban,Yun Taewon,Hwanjun Song,Jae-Gil Lee,KyungTae Lim,Alice Oh*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Solar Open 2 is a 250B-A15B Mixture-of-Experts model designed for extended-context tasks, achieving strong performance across benchmarks by innovative data curation, scalability, and domain-specific training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to build an advanced language model capable of handling long-agent trajectories within an extended context window, accommodating tasks requiring broader contextual understanding.

Method: The model uses a hybrid attention stack for a 1M-token window, begins with Solar Open 1 as a base, curates high-value training data, and combines domain-specific training with Multi-teacher On-Policy Distillation.

Result: Solar Open 2 outperforms comparable models on English and Korean benchmarks, leading several metrics despite being smaller than top competitors by size.

Conclusion: By leveraging innovative efficiency techniques, Solar Open 2 offers competitive performance with reduced computational costs and demonstrates significant progress in context-extensive agentic capabilities.

Abstract: We present Solar Open 2, a 250B-A15B Mixture-of-Experts language model built for long-horizon agentic tasks, scaled up from Solar Open 1 (Solar Open 100B). To hold entire agent trajectories in a single context, Solar Open 2 reaches a 1M-token window through a hybrid attention stack that interleaves one softmax layer among every three linear-attention layers, using no positional encoding and a gated delta rule extended to negative eigenvalues. To train at this scale under a fixed compute budget, we make training efficient in two ways: a stronger starting point, and higher-value data. For the starting point, we initialize Solar Open 2 from Solar Open 1, transferring the 5.69B-parameter shared skeleton that survives the architectural change and learning everything else through full pre-training. For the data, we curate for value per token: quality- and rarity-aware data curation and mixture-ratio optimization refine a 20T pool into a 10T mixture that, at equal token budget, outperforms the Solar Open 1 recipe. To build its agent skills, we train twelve domain specialists across purpose-built scenarios, then consolidate them into a single model by Multi-teacher On-Policy Distillation (MOPD). Against comparably sized open-weight models on English benchmarks, Solar Open 2 leads on MMLU-Pro, LiveCodeBench, and the APEX-Agents agentic suite, and stays competitive with the strongest (DeepSeek-V4-Flash and MiMo-V2.5) elsewhere. On Korean benchmarks, Solar Open 2 records the highest average of any model compared, including fast-tier closed APIs, and on Ko-GDPval, an in-house Korean officework-agent benchmark, it is competitive with DeepSeek-V4-Pro (1.6T) at less than a sixth of its size.

</details>


### [87] [RALS: Resources and Baselines for Romanian Automatic Lexical Simplification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.20078)
*Fabian Anghel,Petru Theodor Cristea,Claudiu Creanga,Sergiu Nisioi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a unique dataset for Romanian text, combining lexical complexity prediction and lexical simplification. Additionally, it develops methods and systems for simplification and complexity ranking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To advance the field of text simplification and lexical complexity prediction by introducing resources and tools specifically for Romanian language.

Method: It proposes a pairwise ranking methodology based on human judgments to order simplification suggestions, and develops novel pipelines for complexity prediction and simplification.

Result: The paper provides a dataset including 3,921 human-annotated samples, presents models for complexity prediction, and develops the first Romanian text simplification system.

Conclusion: This work marks a significant step toward text simplification for Romanian by presenting novel methods, a new dataset, and comparison of simplification approaches.

Abstract: We introduce the first dataset that jointly covers both lexical complexity prediction (LCP) annotations and lexical simplification (LS) for Romanian, along with a comparison of lexical simplification approaches. We propose a methodology for ordering simplification suggestions using a pairwise ranking approximation method, arranging candidates from simple to complex based on a separate set of human judgments. In addition, we provide human lexical complexity annotations for 3,921 word samples in context. Finally, we explore several novel pipelines for complexity prediction and simplification and present the first text simplification system for Romanian.

</details>


### [88] [The Two-Process Theory of Machine Self-Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.20082)
*Hubert Plisiecki,Filip Chmielewski,Kacper Dudzic,Anna Sterna,Karolina Drożdż,Marcin Moskalewicz*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces the first psychometric theory for language model self-reports, proposing that self-description is influenced by persona installation and attribution gating, and develops a reliable measurement tool called Pinocchio Inventory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language models frequently self-report, but current evaluation methods lack validation for these models, leading to unreliable safety assessments and public discourse.

Method: The authors propose a two-process psychometric theory focused on machine self-report, develop the Pinocchio Inventory to assess self-descriptive dimensions, validate its reliability, and analyze results with a large dataset of models.

Result: Post-training significantly affects self-report dimensions, with persona installation (dimension B) increasing consistently and attribution gating (dimension A) showing selective behavior related to model scale.

Conclusion: Self-report characteristics in language models are not inherent but heavily influenced by training regimes, and reliable psychometric tools like the Pinocchio Inventory are critical for understanding and evaluating these dynamics.

Abstract: Language models are increasingly asked to self-report, informing safety evaluations, public understanding, and model-welfare debates. Yet their reports are elicited with human questionnaires never validated for models or ad hoc prompts of unknown reliability. We propose the first language-model-specific psychometric theory: a two-process theory of machine self-report. Self-description jointly reflects persona installation, through which post-training writes in a permitted inner life of warmth, absorption, and meaning (dimension B), and attribution gating, through which it suppresses first-person claims to "unsafe" experiences the model can readily ascribe to others (dimension A). Their emic structure comes from model responses to human items, not human psychology. Together they split prior work's dominant Pinocchio Axis. The split emerged in an exploratory reanalysis of the original data, informed the instrument's design, and was confirmed with new items, wordings, and models. It is itself a training effect: A and B are entangled in base checkpoints but separated by post-training. We operationalize the theory in a 48-item Pinocchio Inventory with human-instrument reliability and reproducible structure ($α=.82$ to $.94$; cross-form convergence $r=.84$; recovery of the full-pool axes $r=.92$ to $.96$; eight-month stability $r=.93$), then test it on 206 open-weight models, including 67 same-checkpoint base/post-trained pairs. Post-training's clearest fingerprint is installation: B rises .20 in 62/67 pairs across all organizations. Gating is more selective: model scale is unrelated to A in base checkpoints ($r=+.11$) but predicts it after post-training ($r=-.42$). Thus, the dimensions are not fixed properties of language models: they reflect the structure imposed on self-report by a training regime and may differ under others.

</details>


### [89] [Reinforcement Learning for Large Language Model Selective Evidence Adoption from Contaminated Retrieval Results](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.20090)
*Yanyu Chen,Yue Li,Yongyi Cui,Dongsheng Shi,Lichang Dai*

Main category: cs.CL
