Designed and evaluated four orchestration architectures involving multi-agent pipelines, adversarial debates, hierarchical consultation systems, and hybrid systems.

Result: The hybrid model combining Qwen3-4B and Foundation-Sec-8B achieved 35.30% accuracy, outperforming both cyber-specialized and frontier model baselines. Gemini model remained the strongest with 38.22% accuracy.

Conclusion: Orchestrated ensembles of SLMs can match or surpass individual LLM performance for malware report analysis, with evidence-grounded methods showing significant promise to improve SLM collaboration.

Abstract: Malware analysis demands rapid interpretation of complex detonation reports spanning filesystem, network, and process behaviours. While large language models (LLMs) demonstrate impressive capabilities for technical artifact interpretation, the opacity and escalating API costs of closed-weight frontier models motivate exploration of open-weight alternatives. However, many open-weight models are large, demanding significant compute resources and incurring non-trivial hosting costs that place them beyond reach for resource-constrained deployments. This paper investigates whether orchestrated ensembles of small language models (SLMs) can match or exceed single LLM performance on structured questions about malware detonation reports. We established baselines by testing eleven open-weight SLMs, three cyber security pre-trained models, and six frontier LLMs on Meta's CyberSecEval Malware Analysis benchmark. We then designed and evaluated four orchestration architectures: (i) a multi-agent pipeline that decomposes analysis into structured evidence-collection and reasoning stages, (ii) an adversarial debate framework in which two agents iteratively critique each other's reasoning, (iii) a hierarchical consultation system that pairs a general-purpose SLM with a cyber-specialised expert model, and (iv) a hybrid architecture that combines evidence-grounded pipelines with adversarial debate reasoning. The hybrid system (Qwen3-4B with Foundation-Sec-8B) achieved 35.30% overall accuracy, exceeding the strongest cyber-specialised baseline (22.54%) and the strongest ungrounded frontier baseline (34.77%); when given the same evidence pipeline, grounded Gemini remained the strongest configuration at 38.22%. These findings show that evidence-grounded orchestration can substantially improve the performance of collaborative SLMs for supporting interpretation of malware detonation reports.

</details>


### [404] [The Ethics of Autonomous AI Agents for Offensive Security](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.20255)
*Andreas Happe,Jürgen Cito,Jasmin Wachter*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper discusses the transformative impact of LLM-driven autonomous agents on offensive security, emphasizing their indeterminacy in actions, impacts, and user accessibility, while examining the challenges of moral responsibility and proposing recommendations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the rise of LLM-powered autonomous tools that disrupt traditional offensive security frameworks, necessitating a deeper understanding of their implications and governance.

Method: The analysis focuses on evaluating the indeterminacies of these tools, their impact on stakeholders, and gaps in existing cybersecurity and AI ethics frameworks, along with proposing recommendations.

Result: The research finds that these agents provide a significant advantage to attackers in the short term and identify a potential for democratized defensive practices in the long term. However, dual-use dilemmas complicate moral attribution among stakeholders.

Conclusion: This paper highlights a paradigm shift in offensive security, urging updates to ethical and regulatory frameworks to better address the unique challenges posed by LLM-driven autonomous agents.

Abstract: LLM-driven autonomous agents are reshaping offensive security. Unlike traditional penetration-testing tooling -- deterministic, narrowly scoped, and operated by trained practitioners -- agentic security tools exhibit \textit{indeterminacy} along three independent dimensions. First, their actions are drawn from a non-deterministic policy whose outputs resist both ex-ante and ex-post explanation, frustrating incident attribution and pre-deployment safety review. Second, their impact is open-ended due to the non-deterministic actions, agency of utilized models, and opaque LLM supply-chains. Third, their user population is indeterminate in both size and required skill: the operating skill floor for using or developing offensive capabilities has dropped sharply. These three properties are linked thematically, but are not derivable from one another. Combined with the structural cost asymmetry between offense and defense, they enable the industrialization of offensive capability. The net short-term effect favors attackers, even if the same technology may, in the long run, democratize access to defensive practice. Existing dual-use cybersecurity and AI-ethics frameworks were not designed for this combination. Our work analyzes how moral attribution becomes diffuse between users, tool-makers, and third parties when employing autonomous AI agents for offensive security. We also examine the stakeholder impact of this technology and provide stratified recommendations.

</details>


<div id='stat.ME'></div>

# stat.ME [[Back]](#toc)

### [405] [Deep Shape Regression for Planar Curves with Multimodal Covariates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19600)
*Manuel Pfeuffer,Roshan Prakash Rane,Hadya Yassin,Kerstin Ritter,Sonja Greven*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: This paper introduces a deep shape regression model capable of handling multimodal high-dimensional covariates and irregularly sampled open planar curves while ensuring geometric invariances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding and modeling the geometric properties of planar curves—removing factors like translation, rotation, scale, or parametrization—is critical in applications like neuroimaging for effective analysis.

Method: The authors propose a conditional Procrustes mean-based model where curves are treated as complex-valued functions. They introduce a deep conditional covariance smoother with modality-specific encoders to estimate covariance. An algorithm for elastic mean estimation further improves geometric invariance by iterating multiple alignments.

Result: The model demonstrates strong performance on simulated datasets with known conditional means. It also successfully analyzes hippocampal outlines from the ADNI cohort, yielding covariate effects that align with previous literature.

Conclusion: This model is robust, geometrically invariant, and applicable to multimodal datasets, offering a solution for handling planar curves in scenarios like neuroimaging, with code openly available for further application.

Abstract: The shape of a planar curve is the geometric information that remains once translation, rotation, scale and reparametrisation are removed and is of interest in many health applications, e.g. in neuroimaging. We propose a deep shape regression model for open planar curves that admits multimodal and high-dimensional covariates. Representing curves as complex-valued functions, we show that the conditional full Procrustes mean is the leading eigenfunction of the conditional covariance. To estimate this covariance surface, we propose a novel deep conditional covariance smoother with modality-specific encoders - e.g. splines for scalar covariates and convolutional networks for images, which classical spline smoothers cannot accommodate. Our model is by construction invariant to the translation, rotation and scaling of the input curves and handles sparsely and irregularly sampled curves. We further provide an algorithm for elastic mean estimation that also removes parametrisation by iterating covariance smoothing, rotational alignment and parametrisation alignment. We illustrate the method on simulated outlines with known conditional mean and multimodal covariates, and give a first application to hippocampal outlines from the ADNI cohort, recovering covariate effects consistent with the literature. Code is available at https://github.com/mpff/dnn-shapes.

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [406] [Dreamer-CPC: Message Learning with World Models for Decentralized Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19809)
*Taisuke Takayama,Naoto Yoshida,Tadahiro Taniguchi*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper introduces Dreamer-CPC, a method for decentralized model-based multi-agent reinforcement learning, enabling better message communication and performance by utilizing latent world model dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address communication challenges in multi-agent reinforcement learning under partial observability, where current approaches do not effectively leverage historical information.

Method: Proposes Dreamer-CPC, integrating Collective Predictive Coding (CPC) into the world model of DreamerV3. Each agent independently uses a world model and exchanges messages inferred from latent states that encapsulate past observations and actions.

Result: Dreamer-CPC outperformed IPPO-CPC and no-communication baselines in two tasks, achieving 4-5 times higher episode returns in the CatchApple task where observations were partially missing.

Conclusion: Communication using latent dynamics of world models significantly improves decentralized decision-making under incomplete observation conditions compared to existing methods.

Abstract: In multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), inter-agent communication is effective for improving performance under partial observability. Representation learning-based approaches enable decentralized agents to learn messages grounded in their own observations, but they rely only on current observations and cannot convey information accumulated over time. We propose Dreamer-CPC, a decentralized model-based MARL method that integrates message learning based on Collective Predictive Coding (CPC) into the world model of DreamerV3. Each agent independently maintains a world model and a message module, and infers and exchanges messages from the latent states of the world model that reflect the history of past observations and actions. We evaluated Dreamer-CPC in two environments: Observer, a non-cooperative information-sharing task, and CatchApple, a newly introduced task in which task-relevant observations are temporarily missing. In both environments, Dreamer-CPC outperformed IPPO-CPC, an existing CPC-based method that generates messages from current observations, as well as no-communication baselines. In particular, in CatchApple, Dreamer-CPC achieved 4 to 5 times the episode return of IPPO-CPC, demonstrating effective coordination where other methods fail due to missing observations. These results suggest that communication grounded in the latent dynamics of world models can support decentralized decision-making when current observations alone are insufficient.

</details>


### [407] [Harnessing Disagreement: Detecting Correlated Agreement Blindness in Multi-Agent Triage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.19899)
*Shay Seiya McDonnell,Avantika Singh,Quoc-Viet Pham,Vratislav Havlik,Gregory M. P. O'Hare*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: This paper identifies a structural blind spot in multi-agent arbitration systems where agreement among advanced learners weakens safety monitoring in cases of correlated failures, and presents a new approach called ARAT to address this issue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent arbitration systems rely on disagreement-triggered escalation to ensure safety by detecting potential failures. As base learners improve, they often converge, leading to a phenomenon where correlated failures evade detection, creating a significant safety vulnerability.

Method: The paper proposes ARAT—a system combining inductive Random Forest (RF), analogical case-based k-NN, and a calibrated meta-model. It uses mechanisms such as conservative override and safety-flag gates to mitigate correlated agreement blindness.

Result: On the UNSW-NB15 dataset, ARAT reduced under-predictions significantly, and cross-dataset validation on clinical readmissions confirmed these results. The study shows that improving base learners alone increases error correlation and reduces productive disagreement.

Conclusion: ARAT successfully addresses the issue of correlated agreement blindness in multi-agent arbitration systems, highlighting the importance of balancing model diversification with disagreement-driven safety mechanisms to reduce correlated errors effectively.

Abstract: Disagreement-triggered escalation can create a structural blind spot in multi-agent arbitration: as base learners improve, they tend to converge, weakening safety monitoring where correlated failures concentrate. We term this correlated agreement blindness and present ARAT (Arbitrated Reasoning Agents for Alarm Triage), a directed-star system combining an inductive Random Forest (RF) agent, an analogical case-based k-nearest neighbour (k-NN) agent, and a calibrated meta-model to mitigate this effect. On 82,332 holdout samples from the UNSW-NB15 network intrusion detection dataset, 57.2% of errors occur under agreement and 90.6% of dangerous under-predictions evade disagreement-based monitoring even after conservative override; ablation shows that strengthening base learners increases error correlation while reducing disagreement. ARAT reduces under-prediction relative to soft voting from 4.80% to 1.70% via conservative override (-2.6pp) and a safety-flag gate (-0.5pp), demonstrating architectural gains. Cross-dataset validation on clinical readmission supports these indicators, suggesting that diversification improves safety only when it generates productive disagreement rather than convergence. These results indicate that disagreement-triggered escalation can be blind to correlated failure, a risk that may intensify as agentic pipelines deploy increasingly capable, correlated models.

</details>


<div id='physics.flu-dyn'></div>

# physics.flu-dyn [[Back]](#toc)

### [408] [Hard Guarantees at a Measured Price: Entropy-Stable Learned Finite Volumes for Compressible Flow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.20171)
*Denis Gueyffier*

Main category: physics.flu-dyn

TL;DR: The paper introduces a learned finite volume scheme for solving two-dimensional Euler equations on unstructured meshes, ensuring physical admissibility by construction. It provides an iso-cost comparison against classical baselines, demonstrating nuanced results across varying scenarios and stresses the importance of guarantee mechanisms for robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of learned solvers for compressible flow, such as lack of physical admissibility guarantees and their comparison at fixed mesh resolution rather than fixed computational cost.

Method: Developing a learned finite volume scheme with entropy-stable flux and evaluating it through rigorous protocols including factor decomposition, iso-cost comparisons, and varied controls. Enhancements were addressed post-analysis to improve the scheme's out-of-distribution behavior.

Result: The learned scheme demonstrated robust guarantees, completing all rollouts with zero negativity events and showing nuanced benefits dependent on flow scenarios. At inference time, corrections improved performance in challenging cases without retraining.

Conclusion: The study highlights the importance of enforcing admissibility guarantees for robust results with learned schemes and demonstrates their effectiveness under iso-cost comparisons. The introduced scheme's corrections successfully improved performance, showcasing potential advancements in learned solvers.

Abstract: Learned solvers for compressible flow are usually compared to classical methods at equal mesh resolution rather than at equal computational cost, and they typically offer no guarantee that their solutions remain physically admissible. We present a learned finite volume scheme for the two-dimensional Euler equations on unstructured meshes, admissible by construction and with an entropy-stable interior flux. We evaluate it under protocols fixed before any computation: frozen thresholds, falsification clauses, negative controls, a factor decomposition of the learned components, and an iso-cost comparison against the refined classical baseline. The decomposition produced the central result: the guarantee machinery alone, with both learned heads switched off (the unlearned skeleton), is the strongest scheme at equal mesh on every periodic case. At equal wall-clock cost the picture inverts into a map. Learning pays robustly only on the wall case whose boundary-condition type it never saw (10.8%). Its periodic gains flip sign with the evaluation draw (+10% on one held-out case, -12% on the hardest). The skeleton is the only method whose iso-cost gain never changes sign, at a measured overhead of 1.74x per step. The guaranteed variant completes 36 of 36 rollouts, Mach extrapolation and unseen wall included, with zero negativity events. We fix the guaranteed scheme's one remaining out-of-distribution weakness, Mach extrapolation, at inference time: with scale-invariant network inputs, a specific-entropy floor, and no retraining, the corrected arm overtakes the unconstrained arm on one Mach case, cuts its deficit on the other by a third, passes the skeleton on the unseen wall, and keeps the guarantee. A spatial gate closes the loop: activating the heads only near the walls beats both the skeleton and the corrected arm, and transfers unchanged to a second wall geometry.

</details>


### [409] [Label-Free Finite-Volume-Residual Training of Attention Graph Neural Networks for Coupled Thermo-Fluid Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.20321)
*Tianyu Li,Zhiwei Cao,Qingang Zhang,Ruihang Wang,Binyang Song,Yonggang Wen*

Main category: physics.flu-dyn

TL;DR: The study focuses on training attention graph neural networks (GNNs) for predicting 3D thermo-fluid fields without relying on labeled data, using finite-volume method (FVM) residuals as a training signal. In experiments, this approach was both accurate and cost-efficient compared to traditional supervised training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation behind this study is to address the computational and storage costs associated with generating labeled data for training neural networks that predict 3D thermo-fluid fields. By introducing a method that eliminates the need for labeled data, model training becomes more scalable and resource-efficient.

Method: The method involves training an attention-based graph neural network (GNN) by minimizing residuals from the finite-volume method (FVM), evaluated directly on the mesh. This eliminates the need for labeled training data, as the residuals serve as a self-supervised training signal.

Result: The FVM-loss-trained models achieved low normalized root-mean-square errors (2.3-2.8%) on steady-state benchmarks and showed close agreement with CFD references, including capturing complex phenomena like buoyancy-energy coupling. In transient cases, the approach outperformed supervised models in accuracy, avoiding the costs of data generation.

Conclusion: Utilizing FVM residuals as a training signal for neural surrogates is an effective and practical approach, delivering competitive accuracy while significantly reducing computational costs and storage requirements.

Abstract: Neural surrogates are widely used in scientific machine learning for fast prediction of three-dimensional (3D) thermo-fluid fields. However, generating training data using conventional numerical solvers often incurs substantial computational and storage costs. We propose to train an attention graph neural network by minimizing the finite-volume method (FVM) residuals of the governing equations. These residuals are evaluated directly on the mesh, requiring no labeled data. We evaluate the trained surrogates against computational fluid dynamics (CFD) references and a data-supervised baseline across four scenarios. On the two steady-state benchmarks, the FVM-loss model achieves an all-field normalized root-mean-square error (nRMSE) of 2.3-2.8%. It demonstrates close agreement with the CFD references, including the buoyancy-energy coupling. On the two parametric transient cases, the FVM-loss model outperforms the supervised baseline in terms of accuracy, while avoiding the data-generation cost entirely. These results indicate that the FVM loss can provide a practical training signal for neural surrogates and reduce the model development cost.

</details>
